fa: Found in 1912 by a German expedition; Egypt has demanded its return ever since. The painted left eye was intentionally left blank — scholars still debate why
One of the most vivid faces from the ancient world; its inlaid quartz eyes still seem alert after 4,500 years — visitors report feeling watched
One of the oldest known sculptures at just 11 cm tall; its exaggerated form suggests a fertility symbol — possibly the earliest surviving work of art
The largest monolith statue in the world at 73 meters long; the missing nose was blamed on Napoleon's soldiers but drawings from 1737 already show it broken
Over 8,000 soldiers each with a unique face; accidentally discovered in 1974 by farmers digging a well — the artisans were reportedly entombed alive to keep the secret
The 1,000 moai were 'walked' upright using ropes — proven experimentally in 2012; the largest weighs 82 tonnes and was never successfully erected
The 17 known heads weigh up to 40 tonnes and were carved without metal tools; the nearest basalt source is 150 km away — transport remains a mystery
The most famous ancient Greek statue; its missing arms have been debated for two centuries — some believe she held a shield or an apple
Considered the greatest surviving work of Hellenistic sculpture; Nike landing on a ship's prow, wings still spread in triumphant flight
Called the 'prototype of human anguish in art' by Michelangelo; depicts the Trojan priest and his sons strangled by sea serpents
The discus thrower frozen at the peak of his throw — one of the first works to capture dynamic movement rather than static pose
The 'Canon' — Polykleitos's mathematical ideal of perfect human proportions, so influential it was called 'The Rule' across Greece
One of the best-preserved bronze statues from antiquity; the inlaid glass-and-copper eyes still glint as if alive after 2,500 years
The definitive portrait of Roman imperial power; Cupid riding a dolphin at his feet hints at Augustus's divine descent from Venus
The symbol of Rome: the she-wolf suckling Romulus and Remus; modern analysis shows the twins were added in the 15th century by Renaissance sculptors
Found at sea in 1928; scholars debate whether the arm hurls a thunderbolt (Zeus) or a trident (Poseidon) — the god's identity remains unknown
The first major Greek sculpture showing gods with human emotion; Hermes teases the infant Dionysus with grapes — and the child reaches for them
A powerful expression of dignity in defeat; the wounded warrior chooses to die with honor — Romantic poets wrote elegies to him
The massive hero leans exhausted on his club after the Labors; the apples of the Hesperides hidden behind his back show the final task is done
An athlete scraping oil and dust from his arm; Lysippos introduced a new body canon — seven-and-a-half head heights — that endured for centuries
At 5.17 meters, David is shown not after victory but before battle — tense, calculating, ready. The defining image of the Renaissance ideal of human potential
Michelangelo's only signed work, completed at 24; the serenity of Mary's face holding the dead Christ has moved viewers to tears for 500 years
So lifelike that Michelangelo struck the knee demanding 'Why dost thou not speak?'; the horns are from a mistranslation of 'rays of light' in the Bible
Michelangelo's first large-scale work, made at 21; Bacchus's slightly tilted stance and unfocused gaze perfectly convey inebriation in stone
Intended for Pope Julius II's tomb; the sensual figure surrenders to death — or sleep — in one of history's most serene depictions of dying
The first freestanding nude male statue since antiquity; shown after the battle — serene, hat shading a knowing smile, Goliath's head at his feet
The first life-size equestrian bronze since antiquity; the mercenary commander's calm face radiates authority — Donatello studied Roman imperial sculpture to achieve it
Shockingly realistic: a gaunt, wild-haired woman in rags; completely unlike ideal Renaissance beauty — a direct emotional confrontation with mortality and sin
Michelangelo called them worthy of being the Gates of Paradise; 27 years of work in 10 gilded panels that established perspective relief sculpture for two centuries

n: Perseus with the Head of Medusa
fa: Cast in a single night in a dramatic furnace crisis Cellini described in his autobiography; Michelangelo declared it the most beautiful work in the piazza
Unlike Donatello's or Michelangelo's, Bernini's David is caught mid-action — the sling swings, the jaw clenches; Bernini used his own face, biting his lip
The angel's smile and Teresa's upturned face create an ambiguity between spiritual ecstasy and earthly rapture that shocked and fascinated in equal measure
Bernini captured the impossible: the exact instant Daphne's fingers sprout leaves and her skin becomes bark — all in cold marble
Pluto's fingers visibly compress Proserpina's thigh — visitors refuse to believe it is stone; carved when Bernini was just 23 years old

lo: V&A Museum, London / Hermitage, St. Petersburg
fa: Two versions exist; when Britain and Russia both claimed ownership, Canova carved a second — the dispute over which is 'better' continues among scholars
The arms of Cupid form a perfect diamond; Napoleon acquired it for himself — Canova had to carve a second version for the original patron
Carved when Voltaire was 83 and dying; the philosopher's ironic smile, hooded gaze, and enormous wig form the defining image of the Enlightenment
Mark Twain called it 'the most mournful and moving piece of stone in the world'; it memorializes 786 Swiss Guards massacred defending Louis XVI in 1792
Originally conceived as Dante contemplating Hell for the Gates of Hell; now a universal symbol of philosophy, introspection, and human thought
The couple are frozen at the moment before their first kiss — they never actually touch. Inspired by Paolo and Francesca from Dante's Inferno
Rodin refused a pedestal — he wanted the six citizens at street level, among the people. The city insisted on a base; he never forgave them
A commission that consumed 37 years; contains over 180 figures including early versions of The Thinker and The Kiss. Never formally completed
Rejected as scandalous in 1898; the novelist is a massive cloaked form — a force of nature, not a man. Now considered a proto-modern masterpiece
One of the world's most visited (and most attacked) sculptures — beheaded, painted, and blown up multiple times; still sits quietly on her rock
The only sculpture Degas exhibited in his lifetime; shown with real hair and a tutu, it shocked the art world — critics called it 'a wax doll' and 'a zoo specimen'
A total rejection of Rodin's sensuous style: two embracing figures reduced to geometric essentials — the lovers barely distinguishable from the block itself
US customs refused to classify it as art and taxed it as industrial metal; Brâncuși won the lawsuit — a landmark case for abstract art
A sleeping head reduced to an oval — all features dissolved into the smooth curve of a dream; influenced a generation of abstract sculptors worldwide
At 29.35 meters, his tallest work; meant to honor Romanian WWI soldiers and support the sky — he called it 'a staircase to heaven'
The first 'readymade' — Duchamp simply mounted a bicycle wheel on a stool. It changed the definition of art: the concept matters, not craft

n: Unique Forms of Continuity in Space
fa: The defining work of Italian Futurism; a figure dissolving into speed and motion — it appears on the Italian 20-cent euro coin
Moore created dozens of reclining figures throughout his life; the horizontal body echoing landscape became his signature obsession — over 30 versions in total
Placed on the exact spot where the world's first nuclear reactor went critical in 1942; the form simultaneously evokes a skull, a mushroom cloud, and a cathedral dome
The first commissioned mobile in MoMA's permanent collection; each element shifts in air currents creating an ever-changing composition — no two moments are the same
The archetypal lone figure of post-war existential art; its elongated roughness conveys the isolation of modern existence. Sold for $104.3M in 2010
A solitary figure gestures into the void — Giacometti's response to the existential crisis of post-WWII Europe; sold for $141.3M at Christie's in 2015
Five tiny figures cross a vast plaza without interacting; the immense negative space between them defines loneliness as powerfully as any painting
The internal structure uses a palm frond, ceramic jugs, and scrap metal; Picasso found beauty in junk and cast it in bronze — the process became the meaning
A 36-meter curved steel wall generated the most famous public-art controversy in American history — leading to a public hearing and its forced removal in 1989
With a 54-meter wingspan, wider than a Boeing 757; seen by more than one person per second — roughly 33 million people per year passing on the nearby A1 road
Nicknamed 'The Bean' by Chicagoans; 110 tonnes of steel with no visible seams — its surface reflects and distorts city and visitors in one continuous liquid mirror
A 155-meter red membrane filled the Tate's Turbine Hall; named after the satyr flayed alive by Apollo — the skin of the mythological victim stretched enormous
The Orange version sold for $58.4M in 2013, then a record for a living artist; the balloon animal elevated to monumental scale questions the nature of high art
A 4.3-meter tiger shark in formaldehyde; the original decayed and had to be replaced in 2006 — raising philosophical questions about what collectors actually own
A 9-meter spider guarding an egg sac; Bourgeois created it as a tribute to her mother: 'My best friend was my mother — as intelligent, patient, and helpful as a spider'
A gift from France; the torch arm was displayed at the Philadelphia Centennial (1876) to raise funds — visitors paid 50 cents to climb inside
Voted one of the Seven Wonders of the Modern World; struck by lightning multiple times but considered miraculous by locals — 38 meters tall, arms spanning 28 meters
The 55-cm bronze boy has over 1,000 costume changes logged in a register; cities worldwide send outfits as diplomatic gifts — the wardrobe has its own museum wing
Coins thrown in are collected nightly — roughly €1.5 million per year, donated to a charity for the poor. The 1954 film Three Coins in the Fountain started the tradition

lo: Campo Santi Giovanni e Paolo, Venice
fa: Completed after Verrocchio's death by Alessandro Leopardi; the general's fierce expression and aggressive posture set the standard for equestrian monuments for 200 years
Its unfinished state — the figure seems to struggle from the marble — sparked Michelangelo's idea of 'non-finito': the eternal prisoner of the stone he's carved from
Michelangelo worked on this until six days before his death at 88; in this final vision Christ and Mary dissolve into each other — barely distinguishable as two figures
An inflatable Easter toy cast in mirror-polished steel; sold for $91.1M in 2019, setting the record for a living artist — a balloon become immortal and untouchable
A blown-up rendering of the Venus of Willendorf; Koons fused 25,000-year-old symbolism with 21st-century spectacle — 40,000 years of fertility goddess in one shiny balloon

ma: Monofrequency lamps, mirror foil, steel, smoke
fa: A gigantic artificial sun in the Turbine Hall; over 2 million visitors came to lie on the floor and contemplate their reflection — more than any Tate show before or since
Two 30-meter horse-head sculptures — the largest equine sculptures in the world — celebrate the heavy horses that powered Scotland's industrial canals and fields

lo: National Museum of Anthropology, Mexico City
fa: The most naturalistic human figure in pre-Columbian art; the kneeling figure's musculature and posture are so accurate they suggest life studies from actual wrestlers
Six maidens (caryatids) serve as columns on the Erechtheion; one original is in the British Museum (taken by Elgin) — Greece has demanded its return for decades
A small relief from the Athena Nike temple; Nike bends to fasten her sandal and the thin 'wet drapery' technique reveals her form with breathtaking naturalism
Degas's late wax models — never intended for public view — were cast in bronze only after his death; the rough, spontaneous surfaces record his vision nearly going blind
